## TD3 Training Pipeline

The training workflow is organized as a configuration-driven sequence from
scenario specification to held-out evaluation. The purpose of the pipeline is
not only to train the TD3 agent, but to ensure that feature generation,
environment construction, and final performance measurement remain auditable
and reproducible across experimental variants.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[Scenario and feature configuration] --> B[Pre-run validation]
    B --> C[Data loading and chronological split]
    C --> D[Feature generation and train-only fitting]
    D --> E[Training environment construction]
    E --> F[TD3 training loop]
    F --> G[Checkpointing and MLflow logging]
    G --> H[Test environment construction]
    H --> I[Held-out evaluation rollout]
    I --> J[Financial metrics and robustness outputs]
```

### Configuration and Validation

Experiments are specified through external scenario and feature configuration
files. These files determine the dataset, feature subset, reward definition,
environment settings, and optimization hyperparameters without requiring source
code changes. Before training begins, the pipeline validates path availability,
feature definitions, split feasibility, and column compatibility so that
misconfigured experiments fail early rather than consuming training time.

### Training Workflow

The processed training split is used to construct the trading environment with
a continuous action space $a_t \in [-1, 1]$ representing target portfolio
exposure. Observations are composed of the engineered feature vector defined in
Chapter 5, while transaction costs and position changes are handled within the
environment so that they enter the reward seen by the agent.

Training then follows the TD3 procedure described in Chapter 4: experience is
collected into a replay buffer, critics are updated from sampled mini-batches,
the actor is updated with delay relative to the critics, and target networks
track the main networks through soft updates. Exploration is introduced through
action noise during training and removed at evaluation time. Checkpoints and
run metadata are persisted throughout the process to support interruption
recovery and experiment comparison.

### Evaluation and Logging

Final results are computed on a separate evaluation environment built from the
held-out test split. Two output streams are kept distinct throughout: the
training reward, which serves as an optimization diagnostic, and the realized
portfolio return series, from which financial metrics are computed. This
separation is necessary because reward shaping may include penalties or other
terms that should not be interpreted as realized investment performance.

Metrics derived from the test rollout include return, volatility, Sharpe ratio,
Sortino ratio, drawdown, turnover, and related behavioral diagnostics. These
outputs, together with configuration snapshots and supporting artifacts, are
logged through MLflow to preserve traceability across experiments.
